old_claim.note: Bronze Working provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Earlier copper and bronze furnace/casting traditions form metallurgical craft lineage, but they are not a direct ferrous prerequisite for medieval iron and steel smithing.

source_shape.source_locator: History section frames bloomery iron smelting after Bronze Age and copper-smelting contexts, and notes that some early iron may have come from accidental iron-ore introduction in copper-smelting operations.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports bronze/copper metallurgy as historical context for later iron smelting, not as a direct material requirement for medieval ferrous metallurgy.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The retained edge is downgraded to lineage because the advanced_metallurgy_medieval node is scoped to iron and steel process improvement, while the source only links earlier copper/bronze practice to the broader emergence of ferrous smelting.

ontology_before: The graph treated Bronze Working as an enabling capability for medieval advanced ferrous metallurgy.
ontology_after: The graph treats Bronze Working as earlier metallurgical craft lineage while preserving Iron Working and Mining as direct ferrous enablers.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed edge type: bronze_working is now historical_predecessor rather than an enabling prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node is broadened to medieval non-ferrous metallurgy where bronze casting is itself part of the scoped process.
A stronger source shows bronze-working shops directly enabled the medieval iron and steel processes represented by this node.
The graph adds a more specific early_metallurgical_furnaces node that should replace bronze_working on this lineage edge.
short_reason: Bronze working is metallurgical ancestry, not a direct ferrous process enabler.
